chunk_summary: This section outlines a staged diagnostic approach for persistent feeding nonresponse, emphasizing high-yield tests and targeted investigations for common medical causes like anemia, celiac disease, TB, and UTI.
learning_objectives: Understand the staged work-up process for feeding nonresponse in children.
Identify high-yield diagnostic tests for anemia, UTI, and celiac disease.
Apply targeted investigations for TB and HIV based on clinical indications.
Recognize when to escalate care or refer for comprehensive evaluation.
key_facts: Stage A tests (2–4 weeks): Hemoglobin for anemia, stool exam for parasites, and urinalysis with culture for UTI.
Stage B tests (4–8 weeks): IgA anti-tTG for celiac disease; IgG-based tests if IgA deficiency is present.
Follow NTEP pediatric TB algorithms, including chest radiography and NAAT/CBNAAT testing.
HIV testing is recommended for recurrent infections or persistent poor response.
Referral to a higher facility is advised if nonresponse persists beyond 8 weeks or danger signs appear.
reference_quote: For suspected celiac disease, follow ICMR guidance that IgA anti-tTG is the preferred serologic test in children above 2 years.
document_key_topics: Staged diagnostic approach for feeding nonresponse
High-yield tests for anemia, UTI, and celiac disease
Targeted investigations for TB and HIV
Referral and escalation criteria for persistent nonresponse
